Section C: Silver Loading & Premium Alignment (SB 1296)

How CSR defunding and policy changes reshaped the marketplace

Gold/Silver Price Ratio Over Time

2018: Silver loading begins (CSR defunding)
2021: Gold falls below Silver (ARP)

What You Pay vs. What Government Pays

200% FPL, Individual Age 40, Silver Plan

American Rescue Plan (2021–2025): ARP/IRA enhanced subsidies capped enrollee contributions at 0% for ≤150% FPL and lowered contributions across all brackets. Expired after 2025. In 2026, the original ACA schedule returns — contributions rise sharply, and the 400% FPL subsidy cliff is reinstated.
Methodology note: "What You Pay vs. What Government Pays" shows the average net premium and APTC for a 40-year-old individual at 200% FPL purchasing the lowest-cost silver plan, averaged across 15 major Texas counties. Three subsidy schedules are applied: original ACA (2015–2020), ARP/IRA enhanced (2021–2025), and 2026 post-ARP reversion (IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-25 indexed percentages with 400% FPL cliff reinstated). All calculations use actual QHP premiums and FPL guidelines for each plan year.

Section D: $0 Plan Eligibility — The Bottom Line

Modeled $0 bronze eligibility vs. observed \u2264$10 enrollment (all 254 TX counties, age-weighted)

82.3%
of 2026 enrollees modeled eligible for a $0 bronze plan (post-ARP reversion)
2018: Tax Cuts & Jobs Act reduces mandate penalty
2021: American Rescue Plan expands APTC thresholds
2023: SB 1296 premium alignment; IRA extends subsidies through 2025
2026: Enhanced subsidies expire; 400% FPL cliff reinstated
Methodology (v2 — age-weighted, all counties): For each of 254 Texas counties and each plan year, we compute whether an enrollee in a given age group and FPL bracket would qualify for a $0 premium on the lowest-cost bronze plan. We use three premium tiers (ind21 for ages 0–34, ind40 for 35–54, ind60 for 55+) and actual SLCSP/bronze premiums from QHP Landscape files, mapped through per-year county-to-rating-area assignments. The applicable percentage is linearly interpolated within each FPL bracket using the relevant subsidy schedule. Under the independence assumption, the joint (age × FPL) probability is the product of marginals, weighted by each county's enrollment. Three subsidy schedules are applied: original ACA (2015–2020), ARP/IRA enhanced (2021–2025), and 2026 post-ARP reversion (IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-25).

Observed vs. Modeled: The dashed orange line (2022–2025) shows the CMS-reported share of enrollees actually paying ≤$10/month after APTC. This is lower than modeled eligibility because many subsidy-eligible enrollees choose silver or gold plans (with higher premiums but richer benefits) rather than the cheapest bronze. The gap reveals a "take-up gap" — the difference between potential $0 access and actual consumer choices.

2026 projection: Uses actual 2026 premiums with the reverted subsidy schedule, but applies the 2025 enrollment distribution (age and FPL by county) as a proxy. Actual 2026 eligibility may differ as the 400% FPL cliff may cause higher-income enrollees to exit the marketplace.

"Without ARP" counterfactual: Shows modeled eligibility if the pre-ARP schedule (with 400% FPL cliff and higher contribution percentages) had remained in effect for all years — isolating the policy impact of enhanced subsidies.

Methodology & Technical Notes

This section documents how each visualization in the dashboard is constructed — including data sources, calculation logic, key assumptions, and known limitations. It is intended to enable replication and to support informed interpretation of the findings.

Contents
  1. Data Sources & Coverage
  2. Enrollment Trends (Tabs 1 & 2)
  3. Premium Pricing & Rating Areas (Section A & B)
  4. Subsidy Calculations & the Three Eras
  5. Silver Loading & SB 1296 (Section C)
  6. $0 Plan Eligibility Model (Section D) — detailed treatment
  7. Limitations & Caveats

1. Data Sources & Coverage

This dashboard integrates two primary federal data products, supplemented by Texas state legislation and IRS regulatory guidance:

Source Coverage Used For
CMS Open Enrollment Period (OEP) Public Use Files Plan years 2015–2025; county-level for TX, state-level for comparison states Total enrollment, metal level distribution, FPL bracket distribution, age distribution, observed ≤$10 premium metric
CMS QHP Landscape Individual Market Medical Files Plan years 2015–2026; plan-level detail for all TX rating areas Gross premiums by metal level, SLCSP identification, lowest-cost bronze/silver/gold per rating area and scenario
IRS Federal Poverty Level Guidelines 2015–2026 (48 contiguous states) Income thresholds for subsidy calculations
IRS Revenue Procedure 2025-25 2026 plan year Indexed applicable percentage schedule for the post-ARP subsidy reversion
Texas SB 1296 (88th Legislature) Effective 2023 Rating area restructuring context (26 areas → 27 areas); premium alignment policy

CMS PUF field availability notes

Not all fields are available in all plan years. The county-level PUF files transitioned from Excel to CSV format starting in plan year 2020, with corresponding changes to column naming conventions. Specific limitations:

  • FPL distribution: Available 2017–2025 at the county level for Texas. The 2017 files use a coarser bracket structure, and some small rural counties (2–5 per year) suppress FPL data entirely.
  • Age distribution: Available 2017–2025 with consistent age brackets (0–17, 18–25, 26–34, 35–44, 45–54, 55–64, 65+).
  • ≤$10 premium metric: The field Cnsmr_Prm_Aftr_APTC_LTEQ10 is only available in plan years 2022–2025. It counts enrollees whose after-subsidy premium is $10 or less per month.
  • 100–138% FPL subgroup: A finer sub-bracket (100–138% FPL) appears alongside the broader 100–150% bracket starting in 2022. The 100–138% group is a subset of the 100–150% group. Our model uses the 100–150% bracket and excludes the 100–138% field to avoid double-counting.

2. Enrollment Trends (Tabs 1 & 2)

What the charts show

The Texas Deep Dive tab presents three enrollment visualizations — total enrollment over time, metal level distribution, and FPL bracket distribution — all drawn from CMS OEP county-level PUFs aggregated to the state level. The State Comparison tab uses state-level PUF summaries for TX, FL, CA, NY, PA, GA, and NC.

Key terms

  • Metal levels — ACA plans are categorized by actuarial value: Bronze (~60%), Silver (~70%), Gold (~80%), and Platinum (~90%). Catastrophic plans are available only to enrollees under 30 or those with hardship exemptions. Higher metal tiers cover a greater share of expected healthcare costs but carry higher premiums.
  • FPL (Federal Poverty Level) — Income thresholds set annually by HHS. Subsidy eligibility and the size of subsidies are determined by a household's income as a percentage of FPL. For a single individual in 2025, 100% FPL = $15,650/year.

Interpretation notes

Enrollment figures represent plan selections during the Open Enrollment Period and may include individuals who subsequently failed to effectuate coverage (i.e., make their first premium payment). CMS does not publish effectuated enrollment at the county level in the OEP PUFs. The dramatic enrollment growth from 2022 onward reflects both expanded ARP subsidies and, for 2024–2025, the Medicaid unwinding (redetermination), which shifted newly ineligible Medicaid recipients into marketplace coverage with special enrollment periods.

3. Premium Pricing & Rating Areas (Sections A & B)

How premiums are sourced

Gross (pre-subsidy) premiums are extracted from the CMS QHP Landscape Individual Market Medical files for each plan year. For each of Texas's rating areas (26 areas in 2015–2022, 27 in 2023–2026), we identify:

  • Lowest-cost bronze: The cheapest bronze-tier plan available, deduplicated by Plan ID. This represents the minimum-cost coverage option an enrollee could select.
  • Second-Lowest-Cost Silver Plan (SLCSP): The benchmark plan used by the IRS and CMS to calculate the Advance Premium Tax Credit (APTC). Identified by sorting all unique silver plans by premium and selecting the second entry. If only one silver plan exists in a rating area, that plan is the SLCSP.
  • Lowest-cost gold: The cheapest gold-tier plan, for comparison purposes.

Six household scenarios

Premiums are reported for six scenarios reflecting age-adjusted rates under the ACA's 3:1 age rating band:

ScenarioDescriptionAge Factor (approx.)
ind21Individual, age 211.000 (base rate)
ind40Individual, age 40~1.278
ind60Individual, age 60~2.714
fam21Family of 4, adults age 21Varies by insurer
fam40Family of 4, adults age 40Varies by insurer
fam50/fam60Family of 4, adults age 50/60Varies by insurer

Rating area mapping

Texas assigns each of its 254 counties to a geographic rating area. Insurers set premiums at the rating area level, so all counties in the same area face the same base rates. A major restructuring occurred in 2023 under SB 1296 (88th Texas Legislature), which reorganized the state from 26 to 27 rating areas and reassigned 209 of 254 counties. This dashboard handles both eras by maintaining per-year county-to-rating-area mappings, extracted directly from the QHP Landscape files.

Why this matters: A county that was in Rating Area 1 from 2015–2022 may have moved to Rating Area 14 in 2023. Naively applying a single mapping would assign incorrect premiums for half the time series. The dashboard uses year-specific mappings to ensure pricing accuracy across the structural break.

Net premium calculation in Sections A & B

When a user selects a county, scenario, and FPL level, the calculator computes the after-subsidy premium using the formula described in Section 4 below. Section B repeats this for all six household scenarios simultaneously using the same county and FPL selection.

4. Subsidy Calculations & the Three Eras

The APTC formula

The Advance Premium Tax Credit (APTC) is the core federal subsidy that lowers premiums for eligible marketplace enrollees. The calculation proceeds as follows:

1. Expected Contribution = Annual Income × Applicable Percentage ÷ 12
2. APTC = max(0, SLCSP − Expected Contribution)
3. Net Premium = max(0, Gross Premium − APTC)

The applicable percentage determines how much of their income a household is expected to contribute toward the benchmark (SLCSP) premium. It varies by income bracket and is linearly interpolated within each bracket. Three distinct schedules have been in effect:

Schedule 1: Original ACA (2015–2020)

FPL RangeApplicable % (start)Applicable % (end)
100–133%2.00%2.00%
133–150%3.00%4.00%
150–200%4.00%6.40%
200–250%6.40%8.10%
250–300%8.10%9.60%
300–400%9.60%9.60%
>400%No subsidy (400% FPL cliff)

Schedule 2: ARP/IRA Enhanced (2021–2025)

The American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 (ARP) dramatically lowered applicable percentages and eliminated the 400% FPL cliff, extending subsidies to all income levels. The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) extended these enhancements through 2025.

FPL RangeApplicable % (start)Applicable % (end)
100–150%0.00%0.00%
150–200%0.00%2.00%
200–250%2.00%4.00%
250–300%4.00%6.00%
300–400%6.00%8.50%
>400%8.50%8.50%

Schedule 3: Post-ARP Reversion (2026+)

Beginning in plan year 2026, the enhanced subsidies expire. The applicable percentage schedule reverts to the original ACA structure, indexed for inflation per IRS Revenue Procedure 2025-25. The 400% FPL cliff is reinstated.

FPL RangeApplicable % (start)Applicable % (end)
100–133%2.10%2.10%
133–150%3.14%4.19%
150–200%4.19%6.60%
200–250%6.60%8.44%
250–300%8.44%9.96%
300–400%9.96%9.96%
>400%No subsidy (400% FPL cliff reinstated)

Within-bracket interpolation

For an enrollee at X% FPL within a bracket spanning [min%, max%], the applicable percentage is:

Applicable % = Start% + (X − min) / (max − min) × (End% − Start%)

For example, an enrollee at 175% FPL under the ARP schedule: bracket is 150–200%, so position = (175−150)/(200−150) = 0.5, and applicable % = 0.00% + 0.5 × (2.00% − 0.00%) = 1.00%.

FPL dollar amounts

The Federal Poverty Level for a single individual (48 contiguous states) by plan year:

2015: $11,770 · 2016: $11,880 · 2017: $12,060 · 2018: $12,140 · 2019: $12,490
2020: $12,760 · 2021: $12,880 · 2022: $13,590 · 2023: $14,580 · 2024: $15,060
2025: $15,650 · 2026: $16,300 (projected)

5. Silver Loading & SB 1296 (Section C)

Gold/Silver price ratio

This chart tracks the ratio of the lowest-cost gold premium to the lowest-cost silver premium for a 40-year-old individual, averaged across all Texas rating areas. A ratio below 1.0 means gold plans are cheaper than silver plans — an inversion caused by "silver loading."

What is silver loading?

In October 2017, the Trump administration ended federal Cost-Sharing Reduction (CSR) payments to insurers. CSRs reduce out-of-pocket costs for enrollees at 100–250% FPL and are only available on silver plans. Rather than absorb the cost, insurers loaded the unpaid CSR expense onto silver premiums specifically. Because the APTC benchmark (SLCSP) is a silver plan, this inflated silver premiums increased subsidy amounts for all enrollees — creating a "silver loading" windfall that made bronze and gold plans effectively cheaper after subsidies.

SB 1296 (2023): Texas passed SB 1296 during the 88th Legislature, which restructured rating areas and included "premium alignment" provisions affecting how insurers distribute CSR costs. The impact is visible in the gold/silver ratio beginning in plan year 2023.

"What You Pay vs. What Government Pays" chart

This stacked area chart shows the monthly cost breakdown for a specific reference scenario:

  • Scenario: 40-year-old individual at 200% FPL purchasing the lowest-cost silver plan
  • Geography: Averaged across 15 major Texas counties (Harris, Dallas, Tarrant, Bexar, Travis, Collin, Denton, Fort Bend, Hidalgo, El Paso, Montgomery, Williamson, Cameron, Nueces, Brazoria)
  • "Consumer Pays" = Net premium after APTC
  • "Government Pays" = APTC amount
  • The subsidy schedule applied follows the three eras described in Section 4
Note: This chart illustrates the subsidy mechanics for a single representative scenario. It is not a population-weighted average. Different age, income, and plan selections would produce different splits.

6. $0 Plan Eligibility Model (Section D)

This is the most methodologically complex component of the dashboard. It estimates the share of Texas marketplace enrollees who could obtain a plan with a $0 monthly premium — reflecting potential access, not actual consumer choices.

6.1 Overview

Section D answers: "What share of marketplace enrollees have access to a free (zero-premium) plan?" It presents three data series:

  • Modeled $0 Bronze Eligibility (green line) — Our model's estimate of the percentage of enrollees who could obtain a $0 premium on the lowest-cost bronze plan, given their county, age, and income.
  • "Without ARP" Counterfactual (red dashed line) — The same model, but applying the pre-ARP subsidy schedule to all years. This isolates the causal impact of enhanced subsidies.
  • CMS Observed ≤$10/month (orange triangles, 2022–2025 only) — The actual share of enrollees paying $10 or less per month after APTC, as reported in CMS PUF data.

6.2 The modeling approach

The model computes eligibility for each of Texas's 254 counties independently, then aggregates to a statewide figure weighted by county enrollment. For a single county in a single year:

Step 1: Map age groups to premium tiers.

CMS Age GroupPremium Scenario UsedRationale
0–17, 18–25, 26–34ind21 (Age 21 rate)ACA child rates apply for dependents; young adult rates are close to the base rate
35–44, 45–54ind40 (Age 40 rate)Mid-range of the 3:1 age rating band
55–64, 65+ind60 (Age 60 rate)Upper end of the age rating band

Step 2: Assign FPL bracket midpoints. Each enrollee's FPL bracket is represented by its midpoint for the subsidy calculation:

FPL BracketMidpoint UsedNotes
<100% FPL75%Below subsidy eligibility floor; these enrollees receive no APTC
100–150%125%Primary bracket for eligibility analysis
150–200%175%
200–250%225%
250–300%275%
300–400%350%Upper bracket; subject to 400% cliff under pre-ARP and post-ARP rules
>400%500%No APTC under pre-ARP or post-ARP; capped contribution under ARP
Other/Unknown200%Conservative default; assigned to the median bracket

Step 3: Compute net premium for each (age, FPL) cell. For each combination of age tier g and FPL bracket f in a county:

1. Look up Lowest Bronze and SLCSP for that county's rating area, using scenario g
2. Compute annual income = FPL_dollar_amount × midpoint(f) / 100
3. Look up Applicable Percentage for midpoint(f) under the relevant subsidy era
4. Expected Contribution = income × applicable_pct / 12
5. APTC = max(0, SLCSP − Expected Contribution)
6. Net Premium = max(0, Lowest Bronze − APTC)
7. If Net Premium ≤ 0 → this cell is "$0-eligible"

Step 4: Weight using the independence assumption. CMS provides age and FPL distributions as separate marginal distributions, not as a joint cross-tabulation. We assume statistical independence between age and FPL within each county:

P(age=g, fpl=f) ≈ P(age=g) × P(fpl=f)

where P(age=g) = count_in_age_group_g / total_age_classified
and   P(fpl=f) = count_in_fpl_bracket_f / total_fpl_classified

Step 5: County-level aggregation.

County Eligibility Rate = Σg,f [ P(age=g) × P(fpl=f) × I(net_premium(g,f) ≤ 0) ]

where I(·) is the indicator function (1 if true, 0 if false)

Step 6: Statewide aggregation.

Statewide Rate = Σcounty [ Eligibility_Rate(county) × Enrollment(county) ] / Σcounty Enrollment(county)

6.3 The counterfactual

The "Without ARP" line applies the pre-ARP subsidy schedule (Section 4, Schedule 1) to all years, including 2021–2025, while keeping actual premiums and enrollment distributions unchanged. This isolates the policy effect: the vertical gap between the green and red lines represents the additional share of enrollees who gained $0 access specifically because of the ARP/IRA subsidy enhancements.

For 2017–2020, the modeled and counterfactual lines are identical (both use the pre-ARP schedule). For 2026, the modeled line uses the post-ARP schedule (Schedule 3) while the counterfactual continues to use the pre-ARP schedule (Schedule 1). Note that the 2026 post-ARP rates are slightly higher than the original pre-ARP rates due to inflation indexing, so the counterfactual may modestly exceed the modeled estimate in 2026.

6.4 Why the modeled rate exceeds the observed rate

The most striking feature of Section D is the gap between modeled eligibility (82–91% during ARP) and observed ≤$10 enrollment (38–62%). This gap is expected and interpretable:

  • The model measures potential access. It asks: "Could this person get a $0 plan if they chose the cheapest bronze?" Many enrollees who could get a free bronze plan instead choose silver or gold plans with higher premiums but richer benefits (lower deductibles, lower copays, prescription drug coverage).
  • The CMS metric measures actual choices. The ≤$10 field counts enrollees who actually selected plans costing $10 or less per month. It captures realized take-up, not theoretical eligibility.
  • The "take-up gap" between modeled and observed is itself a policy-relevant finding. It suggests that many enrollees are willingly paying for richer coverage when they have the option of free minimum coverage — which may indicate the marketplace is functioning as intended, with consumers making informed trade-offs between cost and coverage generosity.
Policy interpretation: The modeled rate answers "How many could get free coverage?" while the observed rate answers "How many chose effectively free coverage?" Both are important — the first measures the reach of the subsidy system, the second measures consumer behavior within it.

6.5 The 2026 projection

The 2026 data point is a projection, not an observation:

  • Premiums: Actual 2026 QHP premiums from the Landscape file (filed and approved rates)
  • Subsidy schedule: Post-ARP reversion (Schedule 3), with 400% FPL cliff reinstated
  • Enrollment distribution: Uses the 2025 county-level enrollment data (age and FPL distributions) as a proxy, since 2026 enrollment data is not yet available

This projection assumes the 2025 enrollment population remains constant, which is unlikely: the reinstatement of the 400% FPL cliff will likely cause some higher-income enrollees to drop marketplace coverage, and Medicaid redetermination dynamics may continue to shift enrollment composition.

6.6 Key assumptions and their justification

AssumptionJustificationPotential Bias
Independence of age and FPL CMS only publishes marginal distributions, not cross-tabs. Independence is a standard econometric approach when joint data is unavailable. In practice, older enrollees may skew toward different income levels. If age and income are positively correlated, the model may slightly overestimate eligibility (older + higher-income cells would be underweighted).
FPL bracket midpoints represent the bracket Without individual-level data, midpoints are the standard simplification for grouped data. Within any bracket, the actual income distribution may be skewed (e.g., toward the lower bound in the 100–150% bracket). This could modestly affect results near the $0 eligibility threshold.
Lowest bronze = relevant plan for $0 analysis The lowest-cost bronze plan sets the floor for what a consumer can access at minimum cost. None for the specific question asked. However, some enrollees may not have access to the absolute cheapest bronze if their preferred provider network differs.
Household size = 1 for FPL calculations Individual market pricing is per-person; using single-person FPL amounts with individual premiums is internally consistent. Family enrollees face different premiums and have different FPL thresholds. Family scenarios (fam21, fam40, fam50) are available in Section A/B but are not incorporated into the statewide eligibility model.
2025 enrollment as 2026 proxy Most recent available data; enrollment composition changes gradually between adjacent years. The subsidy cliff may cause attrition among higher-income enrollees, which would increase the eligible share among remaining enrollees. The 2026 projection may therefore be conservative.

7. Limitations & Caveats

Data limitations

  • Enrollment is plan selections, not effectuated coverage. OEP PUF figures include individuals who selected a plan but may not have paid their first premium.
  • 2017 FPL data has gaps. The 2017 PUF files use a coarser income bracket structure. Two small rural counties (De Witt, Kenedy) have suppressed FPL data for that year.
  • Small-county suppression. CMS suppresses data for cells with fewer than 10 enrollees. In very small counties, some age or FPL brackets may be reported as zero when they actually have a small number of enrollees.
  • No individual-level data. County-level aggregates prevent analysis of within-bracket variation or individual enrollment decisions.

Model limitations

  • Independence assumption. The model treats age and FPL distributions as statistically independent within each county. If these are correlated (e.g., older enrollees systematically have higher incomes), the joint distribution estimates will contain error.
  • Single-person focus. The eligibility model uses individual (household size = 1) premiums and FPL levels. Family households face different premium structures and FPL thresholds that are not captured in the statewide model.
  • Static enrollment for 2026. The 2026 projection assumes unchanged enrollment composition, but the subsidy cliff will likely alter the income and age mix of enrollees.
  • Cost-sharing reduction effects not modeled. Silver plan CSR variants (73%, 87%, 94% actuarial value) effectively reduce out-of-pocket costs for low-income enrollees but are not reflected in the premium-based eligibility calculation.

Scope limitations

  • This dashboard covers the individual marketplace only (Healthcare.gov plans for Texas). It does not include employer-sponsored coverage, Medicaid, CHIP, Medicare, or off-exchange individual plans.
  • Comparison states (Tab 2) use state-level CMS summaries and are not subject to the same county-level analysis as Texas.
  • All monetary values are nominal (not inflation-adjusted). The premium trends reflect both underlying cost changes and general inflation.
Data source: CMS Open Enrollment Period Public Use Files (2015–2026) · QHP Landscape Individual Market Medical Files (2015–2026) · Dashboard generated March 2026
